# Chapter 4: Return to Rome

**Applying Guy Sclanders' Methodology**

## Session Overview

The party enters Rome — as heroes, fugitives, or something in between. Senator Brutus has been operating in the shadows since Session 1. He's visible now. They have less than 24 hours before the Emperor's Triumph turns the streets of Rome into the perfect cover for an assassination.

**Level:** 6 → Advance to 7 at session end
**Duration:** 3–4 hours
**Key Goal:** Identify and stop Brutus' plan before the Triumph
**Theme:** Duty vs. Ambition — Brutus crystallises this. He is what happens when ambition replaces duty.

### Guy Sclanders' Principles Applied

**Never Present Villain, Now Revealed:** Brutus has been the force behind every problem since Session 1. This session he steps forward. Everything the party suspected is true. He's also genuinely compelling — he might even be right about Rome's corruption.

**Three Clue Rule:** Three independent paths to understanding Brutus' full plan before the Triumph.

**Escalation:** Personal → Fort → Regional → **Imperial**. Rome itself is the stakes now.

---

## Pre-Session Preparation

### Tracking Sessions 1–3

| Question | Session 4 implication |
|---|---|
| Was the spear destroyed? | Brutus' cult is attempting to reconstitute it (Scene 3). If the spear exists, he wants it now. |
| Is Tribune Lucius alive? | He can provide direct Senate access and knows Brutus personally. Major asset. |
| Is Cassia alive? | She can perform augury in Rome, giving divine intelligence. |
| What is Vercingetorix's status? | He won't come to Rome, but if alive and allied, he provides a message in Scene 1. |
| Did the Legate survive? | He may have already reported to the Senate — the party's reputation in Rome depends on this. |

### The City of Rome, 175 AD

Before describing anything else, establish what Rome feels like:

> *Fourteen hills. A million people. The smell of it hits before you see it — bread, smoke, animal waste, incense, and the Tiber, always the Tiber, copper-green and cold. The sound is constant: carts banned in daytime traffic means the night never fully sleeps, and the day is voices, hammering, animals, arguments, vendors, soldiers, priests.*
>
> *The Capitoline Hill gleams white above everything. The Senate House. The Temple of Jupiter. The Forum below it, Rome's beating heart.*
>
> *Everything here has a price. Information, access, safety, testimony. The question is who's paying for what.*

Players should feel that Rome is simultaneously magnificent and dangerous. It is the most powerful city in the world and also a place where people disappear.

### Props & Handouts

**Handout 8: Senator Brutus' Correspondence (archive copy)**
```
Found in Senate financial records (DC 15 Investigation):

PRIVATE CORRESPONDENCE — SEAL OF G. CASSIUS BRUTUS

"The frontier matter is resolved. Our man has instructions.
The artifact will not complicate the Triumph as feared.
I trust to your discretion in the matter of our witnesses —
loose threads are the enemy of all great projects.

Regarding the Triumph itself: the arrangements are complete.
The instrument is consecrated. What was broken will be made
whole again in time for the procession.

Roma semper resurget."
        — G.C.B.

[Note in margin, different handwriting: "Witnesses = the party."]
```

**Handout 9: The Triumph Programme (public propaganda)**
```
TRIUMPH OF IMPERATOR MARCUS AURELIUS
Honoring victories on the Germanic frontier
THE KALENDS OF THE MONTH OF MARS

PROCESSIONAL ORDER:
Dawn: Sacrifice at the Temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus
First hour: Senate in formal procession, Capitoline to Forum
Second hour: Military prisoners displayed (Germanic chieftains)
Third hour: The Emperor's chariot, via Sacra
Fourth hour: Games, Circus Maximus — open to all citizens

[Scrawled note from an informant at the bottom:]
"Brutus' villa: unusual traffic last night. Four men in dark
cloaks. Carrying something long. Wrapped."
```

**Physical Props:**
- A small map of Rome's seven hills (easily drawn on a napkin)
- A coin (real or prop Roman coin if available) to pass to the party as Brutus' "gift"

### Reactive World Setup

| NPC | Status | What they're doing in Rome |
|---|---|---|
| Senator Brutus | Moving fast — the Triumph is tomorrow | Finalising the assassination plan; has the replica spear; believes himself Rome's last hope |
| Tribune Lucius *(if alive)* | In Rome but in a difficult position | Can get the party Senate access but is personally implicated in everything; looking for an exit |
| Augur Cassia *(if alive)* | Has performed three auguries since the grove | Receiving visions of blood at the Emperor's triumph — she's been trying to warn someone, but no one in Rome will receive her |
| Legate Corvinus *(if alive)* | Filed a report blaming the party | Working through legal channels to recover his reputation; might cooperate if offered a way to save face |
| Vercingetorix *(if alive)* | Outside the city walls | Won't enter Rome. Sends a message: *"The god is angry. The city smells of his anger. Whatever you do, do it today."* |

---

## Scene 0: Cold Open (5 minutes)

**Start in media res — something is already wrong.**

The party arrives at Rome's outskirts on the Appian Way. They're met on the road before they reach the gates.

**Read aloud:**

> *The Appian Way. Lined with tombs, the way Roman roads always are — the dead watching you enter the city.*
>
> *Your escort from the last post, a single rider named Gaius who joined you two days ago, is lying face-down in the road twenty feet ahead. His horse grazes nearby, unbothered.*
>
> *He's not dead. Just unconscious, with a careful bruise at the base of his skull. Tucked under his arm: a note, sealed in plain wax. No sigil.*

**The note (no envelope, just folded paper):**

*"Leave the matter of the spear alone and live. The Triumph is not your business. The men who started this are prepared to finish it. You are not prepared for what finishing it will cost."*

*No signature.*

**DM Direction:** Don't let this stop them. It should make them move faster. Someone in Rome knows exactly when and how they arrived. The city already has eyes on them.

---

## Scene 1: Entering Rome

The party needs allies, intelligence, and somewhere safe to operate from in the next 24 hours. Three options immediately available:

**Senator Marcus Tullius** — the Brutus opposition

A moderate Senator, genuinely alarmed by what's happening. He can be found at the Temple of Saturn, where he regularly meets clients.

> *He's a tired man in his sixties. He looks at the party the way a man looks at his last hope.*
>
> *"I know why you're here. Brutus has been building to this for three years. He believes the Republic is salvageable if enough blood is spilled in the right places. He's wrong about the blood. He may not be wrong about Rome."*

He offers: Senate access, a safe house, and a private route to the Triumph. In exchange: testimony against Brutus before a Senate committee after the Triumph.

**Tribune Lucius** *(if alive)*

He's been in Rome for two days. He looks like a man who hasn't slept.

> *"I tried to stop this quietly. That option is gone. Brutus knows you're coming — he has people at every gate."*

He can provide: direct access to the Senate archives (Handout 8), knowledge of Brutus' villa location, and personal testimony. He wants: immunity from prosecution, which Senator Tullius can provide if the party negotiates.

**Augur Cassia** *(if alive)*

She came to Rome ahead of the party. She's been at the Temple of Apollo for two days, trying to get anyone to listen.

> *"Nobody will hear me. I'm a frontier augur. I have no standing here. But I've performed three auguries since the grove, and they all show the same thing: blood on the processional route. An Emperor who doesn't reach the Colosseum."*

She can provide: divine intelligence on the timeline (Mars' involvement is accelerating), and access to the Temple network (priests who owe her favors).

---

## Three Clue Rule: Understanding Brutus' Full Plan

The party needs to discover: that Brutus has a replica spear, that it's been ritually consecrated, and that the assassination is planned at the moment the Emperor's chariot passes beneath the Arch of Titus (where the crowd is thickest and Praetorian spacing is widest).

**Clue 1: The Senate Archives** (Tribune's access)
DC 15 Investigation, 1 hour:
- Find Handout 8 — Brutus' correspondence
- Cross-reference with Tribune's knowledge: "the instrument is consecrated" = the replica spear
- "Made whole again" = they've completed a ritual to give the replica partial power

**Clue 2: The Cult Temple** (following Brutus' agents)
DC 14 Stealth, or a hired street informant (5 *denarii*):
- Brutus' agents have been moving through the Subura at night
- They lead to a basement below a metalworker's shop: a makeshift temple to Mars, blood-soaked stones, a replica spear on an altar
- The cult members, if captured (4 **Cult Fanatics** CR 2), reveal: the assassination happens during the third hour of the Triumph, at the Arch of Titus

**Clue 3: Cassia's Augury**
If Cassia performs augury at the Temple of Apollo (1 hour, costs a vial of holy water):
- Vision: A man in a Senator's toga, raising a spear in a crowd. The Emperor's face. An arch. Then blood.
- She can identify the arch from the vision — it's distinctive. It's the Arch of Titus.
- She cannot see the attacker's face. But she knows it's not Brutus himself — *"He would never do his own killing."*

---

## Scene 2: The City of Shadows

While gathering intelligence, the party navigates Rome itself. Use any of these optional scenes to fill time and deepen the setting:

**Option A: Confronting Brutus Directly**

He receives them at his villa — and he was expecting this.

> *The atrium is magnificent. Marble floors. A bronze statue of Cincinnatus. He pours wine himself — a gesture of calculated equality.*
>
> *"Sit. You've earned a conversation."*

Brutus is the most dangerous person in this session, and the danger isn't his stat block — it's his argument. He genuinely believes he's saving Rome:

> *"The Emperor is a philosopher playing at general. The legions are loyal to their generals, not to a man who quotes Marcus Aurelius while Rome rots from the Senate outward. I have watched corruption eat this city for thirty years. Thirty years. Someone has to hold the knife."*

He's compelling. He might be right about the problem. He is catastrophically wrong about the solution. DC 16 Insight to catch the thing he won't say: he doesn't believe Rome will be better after. He just needs something to have mattered.

He will not back down from the plan. He will offer them the chance to not be in the way:
> *"Walk away. Go back to your frontier. This is above you."*

**Option B: Following the Evidence**

Investigating the basement cult temple: the ritual space is active. Someone has been performing nightly rites. The replica spear is *almost* as dangerous as the original — not cursed, but attuned to Mars' anger. Anyone touching it must make a DC 14 Wisdom save or feel the pull of divine wrath (not corruption, but rage — disadvantage on Charisma checks for an hour).

4 **Cult Fanatics** (CR 2) and 1 **Gladiator** (the bodyguard, CR 5) if they arrive when cult members are present.

**Option C: Seeking Divine Aid**

Temple of Jupiter, High Priest:
- Augury costs 100 *denarii* or a worthy gift (DC 14 Persuasion to negotiate)
- Jupiter's response through omens: *"The scales must balance. Blood for blood. Ambition for humility. The god of war watches what you do next."*
- Less specific than Cassia, but confirming: Mars is observing. His response to how this ends will define Session 5.

**Option D: The Legate's Complication**

If the Legate survived and filed a report, the Praetorian Guard is looking for the party. A squad of 6 Praetorians (use Veteran stat blocks) attempts to detain them for questioning at some point during Scene 2. This can be:
- Avoided with a DC 15 Stealth or 14 Persuasion (official authority from Senator Tullius helps)
- Turned: The Praetorian commander (DC 17 Persuasion with evidence) can be convinced that the greater threat is Brutus, not the party

---

## Scene 3: The Triumph

Dawn of the Triumph. Rome is packed -- a million people in the streets for the Emperor's parade.

**Read aloud before the processional begins:**

> *You smell it before you see it: crushed rose petals, olive oil on burning torches, sacrificial smoke from the Capitoline that drifts down into the Forum and never quite dissipates. Underneath all of it, a million human bodies pressed together, which smells like nothing except being alive in large numbers.*
>
> *The noise is something else entirely. It starts as crowd murmur -- the particular human hum of people waiting -- and then the first trumpets sound from the Capitoline steps and the sound changes. It becomes a roar. It goes up from a million throats at once, wordless at first, then shaped: "Imperator! Imperator!"*
>
> *You have been in battles. You have been in storms. This is louder than both.*
>
> *The Senate processes first -- old men in white togas with purple borders, moving in a column with the careful dignity of people who know they are being watched. After them: the lictors with their fasces, the priests with their sacred implements, a dozen white oxen garlanded for sacrifice.*
>
> *Then the prisoners. Germanic chieftains in chains, walking the via Sacra with their heads up -- not in defiance, exactly, but with the specific face of men who have decided how they want to be remembered. The crowd jeers. Some of the chieftains don't react. One of them, a young man with a torc around his neck and a fresh scar on his jaw, looks directly at you as he passes.*
>
> *Then the Emperor's chariot. Gold and ivory. The horses are matched white, and they've been trained for exactly this moment: they don't shy from the roar, they move through it like they've always lived in it.*
>
> *Marcus Aurelius stands in the chariot. He is fifty-four years old and looks it -- not frail, but worn, the way a good tool gets worn. He is holding a laurel branch. Behind him, his slave holds a crown of gold over his head and whispers, repeatedly, the traditional words: "Remember, you are mortal."*
>
> *The Emperor looks like he already knows.*

**The Triumph's structure** (this is the countdown clock):

| Time | Event | Party opportunity |
|---|---|---|
| Dawn | Sacrifice at Jupiter's temple | Last chance to reach Jupiter's priest for divine aid |
| First hour | Senate procession | Find and track Brutus in the crowd |
| Second hour | Germanic prisoners displayed | Vercingetorix's kin are in chains — does that change anything? |
| Third hour | **Emperor's chariot, Arch of Titus** | **The assassination attempt** |
| Fourth hour | Games begin | If they failed: chaos; if they succeeded: the aftermath |

**The Arch of Titus:** A bottleneck in the processional route. The crowd is thickest here. Praetorian spacing is widest because of the press of bodies. This is where Brutus' agent is positioned.

**Brutus' agent:** Not Brutus himself — he's in the Senate procession, establishing his alibi. His agent is a gladiator named **Marius Coda** — a man Brutus freed from slavery who believes in his patron completely. He carries the replica spear under a citizen's cloak.

**Marius Coda: Who He Is**

He was a Syrian slave purchased at age twelve by Brutus' household. Brutus freed him at twenty-five, paid for his training at a gladiatorial school in Capua, and watched him win eighteen consecutive bouts. Marius has no memory of freedom before Brutus gave it to him. He does not question the mission -- not because he is stupid, but because gratitude this deep doesn't leave room for doubt.

He believes: the Emperor is weak, the Republic is salvageable, Brutus is the man Rome needs. He is wrong about the Emperor's weakness and may be wrong about the rest. He is not wrong that Brutus freed him when he didn't have to.

He carries the replica spear under a citizen's cloak, wrapped in felt to prevent the faint aura from being felt. He's been at the arch for two hours already, positioned and waiting.

**Sample dialogue if captured:**

When told Brutus' plan will kill thousands: *"One man's death prevents a war. I've seen wars. I've fought in them. You don't get to talk to me about lives."*

When offered a deal for his testimony: *"Brutus trusted me with this. You want me to pay that back by selling him. You don't understand what he gave me."*

When told Brutus used him: *"Everyone uses everyone. At least he was honest about what he needed."*

**Non-lethal capture path:** Marius can be grappled (Athletics DC 15, he actively resists). If restrained and the replica spear taken from him, he stops fighting -- there is nothing left to fight for. He will not escape. He sits on the Triumph's cobblestones and waits to be arrested with the specific dignity of a man who made his choice and knows it.

A living Marius is more valuable than a dead one: he can testify that Brutus gave the mission directly, describe the basement cult rituals in detail, and name every member of Brutus' inner circle who was present at the planning. Offer him honest terms and he is the best witness in Rome.

*Marius Coda: Use **Gladiator** stat block (CR 5), no armor (hidden), +4 initiative from training. He's not evil. He's loyal.*

**Finding Marius in the crowd:**
- DC 15 Perception each round to spot the concealed weapon profile in the crowd
- DC 13 Insight on anyone behaving oddly near the route
- Cassia's augury vision, if gathered earlier, gives the precise location: south side of the Arch, second arch from the left

**The public fight:**
Every action is witnessed by thousands of citizens. The Praetorians will intervene after 3 rounds — as enemies of everyone until the situation is clear. The party can:
- Identify themselves and their authority (DC 14 Persuasion, faster with Tribune's credentials)
- Hold the Praetorians off while dealing with Marius
- Stop Marius non-lethally (grapple/manacle) — which creates a living witness against Brutus

**If Brutus is exposed publicly** (party presents evidence to the Praetorian commander in the moment):
The Praetorians arrest Brutus during the Triumph itself. The most dramatic possible ending — and publicly devastating for Brutus, who valued his reputation above everything.

**If Brutus escapes:**
He's gone within the hour — he has a ship waiting. He's now a fugitive with resources, a plan that almost worked, and a score to settle. Seeds for a sequel.

**If the party reaches the Emperor directly:**

Marcus Aurelius does not panic. He is a Stoic philosopher who spent thirty years on the frontier. When the party approaches his chariot with evidence of an assassination attempt -- however chaotic the surrounding scene -- he evaluates the situation with the same expression he would use to study a military dispatch.

> *He looks at you for a long moment. The crowd roars around him. His chariot has stopped; the horses are being held by the Praetorian outriders. He steps down from the chariot without hesitation -- his slave tries to protest and he waves the man off.*
>
> *Up close: tired eyes, a general's posture, a philosopher's restraint.*
>
> *"Tell me exactly what you know, in the order you learned it, and do not embellish."*

If the party gives him accurate information: He nods twice, once when they mention Brutus' name and once when they describe the Arch of Titus. He turns to the Praetorian commander and gives three quiet orders that the party cannot fully hear. The processional continues. The Arch of Titus is quietly surrounded before the Emperor's chariot reaches it.

He does not thank the party immediately. Three days later, a written message arrives at wherever they're staying: *"Your service is noted and will be remembered. -- M.A."* That's all. But in Rome, a note from the Emperor in his own hand is more valuable than a public ceremony.

**Post-Triumph outcome narration:**

After Scene 3, give the table a single read-aloud paragraph matching what happened:

*If the Emperor was saved and Brutus arrested:*

> *By the time the Triumph ends at the Circus Maximus, Rome knows. Not the details -- not yet -- but the particular rumor-ripple that moves through crowds when something has happened that will be talked about. Men who were watching the games pause and look at each other. Something changed today. They don't know what.*

*If the Emperor was saved and Brutus escaped:*

> *The games proceed. The crowd doesn't know. But at the Senate House, there is a door sealed and two Praetorians stationed outside a chamber where a committee has been meeting for three hours. Somewhere in the city, a very wealthy man is burning correspondence.*

*If the Emperor was wounded:*

> *The Triumph ends early, officially due to an "unforeseen religious irregularity." The city accepts this. Cities absorb bad news in stages. In the barracks, soldiers clean weapons that don't need cleaning. In the Senate, voices are louder than usual.*

*If Brutus' plan fully succeeded:*

> *The streets of Rome are quiet in the particular way that cities go quiet when they don't know how to respond to what just happened. In the Forum, men stand in clusters. No one has gone home. The Emperor's chariot is somewhere on the Palatine, inside walls. Three Senators have locked their doors.*

---

## Scene 2 Pacing Guidance

Scene 2 has four optional encounters. Run a maximum of two. Run Scene 2 for no more than 60 minutes of table time. Here is the decision matrix:

- **Always run Option A (Brutus confrontation)** if the party collected enough evidence to approach him. This is the campaign's thematic heart; don't skip it.
- **Run Option B (cult temple)** if the party needs Clue 2 and hasn't found it another way.
- **Run Option D (Legate's complication)** if the session needs action pacing relief after two heavy dialogue scenes.
- **Skip Option C (Jupiter's temple)** if Cassia is present -- her augury covers the same information more efficiently.

After two optional scenes, cut to Scene 3: The Triumph. The clock is the pressure. The city is already filling.

**Brutus extended sample dialogue:**

When the party implies he's lying about his motives: *"I have no reason to lie to you. You're not important enough to lie to. I'm telling you exactly what I believe because I believe it is the truth."*

When someone points out his plan will cause civil war: *"Yes. And? The alternative is a slow rot that produces the same result in fifty years, except everyone has forgotten what they were fighting for. I prefer the honest crisis to the comfortable decline."*

When he realizes the party is going to stop him: *"I see. Well. I respect this, actually. I was wondering if you had it in you."* -- he does not beg, does not threaten, does not reach for a weapon. He looks at them with what might be admiration. Then he calls for his guards.

---

## Conclusion

**Advance to Level 7**

Whatever happened with the Triumph, one thing is now certain: Mars has been watching everything that occurred in Rome. The spear, the ritual, the assassination attempt, the party's choices — he has been attentive. Session 5 begins with his response.

### Outcome Table

| What happened | Session 5 opening position |
|---|---|
| Emperor saved, Brutus arrested | Party are public heroes — but Mars' anger arrives at the height of their celebration |
| Emperor saved, Brutus fled | Party succeed but the threat isn't eliminated — and Mars doesn't care about human politics |
| Emperor wounded | Political chaos; party blamed first, then exonerated; Mars arrives into a fractured Rome |
| Brutus' plan partially succeeded | The party's standing in Rome is complicated; they face Mars from a weaker position |

---

## DM Notes

### OGAS This Session

**Senator Gaius Cassius Brutus**
- **Objective:** "Restore the Republic" — overthrow the Emperor and install himself as First Citizen
- **Goal (this session):** Execute the assassination during the Triumph; manage the party
- **Agenda:** Charm the party first; offer them what they want; only resort to force if necessary
- **Secret:** He's already promised Mars' power to three other gods if they support his coup. Jupiter, Pluto, Bellona — all have been approached. He doesn't know if any of them accepted.

**Tribune Lucius Valerius Maximus** *(if alive)*
- **Objective:** Earn respect as a "real Roman"
- **Goal (this session):** Survive, be useful, and come out of this with something left
- **Agenda:** Help the party stop Brutus — not because it's right, but because it's the only path that doesn't end with his own trial
- **Secret:** He told Brutus the party was coming. Two days ago. He regrets it. He's trying to make it right.

### Brutus: Playing the Villain Correctly

Brutus is the most important antagonist in the campaign and must be played with full complexity. The mistake would be to make him a sneering villain who loves evil. He doesn't. He loves Rome. He has watched Rome's Senate decay for thirty years and he believes, sincerely, that the current Emperor — however personally virtuous — represents the end of everything that made Rome Rome.

He is wrong. His method is catastrophically wrong. But he is not lying when he says he wants to save Rome. That's what makes him genuinely threatening and genuinely tragic.

When the party confronts him, let him make his argument. Let them feel the pull of it. Then make them choose to stop him anyway.

That choice — stopping a man who might be right — is peak Sclanders design.

### Three Clue Rule Summary

| | Clue 1 | Clue 2 | Clue 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Information needed** | Archive + Tribune knowledge | Cult temple investigation | Cassia's augury |
| **What it reveals** | The plan exists, spear is consecrated | Location and timing (Arch of Titus, third hour) | Confirmation and that Brutus isn't the triggerman |
| **How found** | Active investigation | Stealth / informant | Divine magic |

Party needs only one to act. All three gives them a significant advantage.

### The Stakes of Witnesses

The party has been creating witnesses and evidence since Session 1. Every NPC they kept alive, every clue they gathered — it all feeds into whether they can stop Brutus legally or are forced into direct action. A party with Lucius alive, Cassia present, and the cult temple evidence can bring Brutus down through Rome's institutions. A party with none of those things has to fight their way through the Triumph.

Both are valid. Both are satisfying. Track what they have.
